title: Hidden Hunger Index vs. share in extreme poverty
subtitle: The Hidden Hunger Index in pre-school children (HHI-PD), which provides an index of three nutritional deficiency estimates (stunting, anemia related to iron deficiency and vitamin-A deficiency) in 2009 versus the share of the population living on less than $3 a day.
note: Poverty data is measured in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices to account for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. Depending on the country and year, it relates to income (measured after taxes and benefits) or to consumption, [per capita](#dod:per-capita).
caption:
Hidden Hunger Index vs. share in extreme poverty

The Hidden Hunger Index in pre-school children (HHI-PD), which provides an index of three nutritional deficiency estimates (stunting, anemia related to iron deficiency and vitamin-A deficiency) in 2009 versus the share of the population living on less than $3 a day.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between the Hidden Hunger Index for pre-school children in 2009 and the share of the population living in extreme poverty in 2012. The vertical axis measures the Hidden Hunger Index from 0 to 50, while the horizontal axis represents the share in extreme poverty from 0% to 80%. Each circle represents a country, with size proportional to population, and colors indicating continental regions: orange for North America, brown for South America, purple for Africa, blue for Europe, teal for Asia, and light blue for Oceania. India appears as the largest teal circle, positioned near a Hidden Hunger Index value of 50 and an extreme poverty share around 33%. China sits lower on the vertical axis near 12 with a poverty share close to 30%. Several African nations, including DR Congo, Madagascar, and Sierra Leone, cluster in the upper-right quadrant with poverty shares exceeding 60% and Hidden Hunger Index values between 40 and 50. Countries like Chile, Russia, and Malaysia occupy the bottom-left area with poverty shares below 10% and index values under 15. A general positive trend is visible, where higher poverty shares correspond to higher Hidden Hunger Index scores. The note specifies that poverty data uses international dollars at 2021 prices to adjust for inflation and cost of living differences, relating to either income or consumption per capita depending on the country.
